attributes.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the Maternity Assessment Unit at 36 weeks gestation with a 24-hour history of reduced fetal movements. This is her first pregnancy. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. The uterus is non-tender on palpation. No fetal heart rate is detected on auscultation. An ultrasound confirms the absence of fetal cardiac activity, consistent with intrauterine fetal death.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This scenario presents a case of stillbirth diagnosed at 36 weeks gestation. The most appropriate next step in management is induction of labour. In cases of confirmed stillbirth, vaginal delivery through induction of labour is generally preferred over caesarean section, unless there are specific maternal indications for surgical delivery.

Immediate caesarean section is not routinely recommended for stillbirth. It carries higher risks of maternal morbidity compared to vaginal delivery and does not improve outcomes in this situation. Caesarean section would only be considered if there were maternal indications such as previous classical caesarean section or placenta praevia.

Expectant management, which involves waiting for spontaneous labour to begin, is an option but is generally not recommended due to the psychological distress it may cause the mother and the increased risk of coagulation disorders with prolonged retention of a dead fetus.

Dilation and evacuation is not appropriate at this gestational age (36 weeks). This procedure is typically used for second trimester pregnancy losses, not for term or near-term stillbirths.

Immediate post-mortem examination is an important part of the investigation into the cause of stillbirth, but it is not the next step in management. The priority is to safely deliver the fetus, after which a post-mortem can be offered and performed if the parents consent.

The management of stillbirth requires a compassionate and multidisciplinary approach. After confirming the diagnosis, the next steps include:1. Providing clear, sensitive communication about the diagnosis and management options.2. Offering emotional support and involving bereavement counselling services.3. Initiating induction of labour, typically using prostaglandins or oxytocin.4. Providing appropriate pain relief during labour and delivery.5. Offering the parents the opportunity to see and hold their baby after delivery, if they wish.6. Collecting samples for investigation (placenta, cord blood, maternal blood tests).7. Discussing and offering post-mortem examination.8. Planning follow-up care and support.
learningPoints: In cases of confirmed stillbirth, induction of labour is generally preferred over caesarean section unless there are specific maternal indications for surgical delivery.
Psychological support and clear, compassionate communication are crucial components of care in stillbirth management.
A thorough investigation into the cause of stillbirth should be initiated, including placental examination, maternal blood tests, and offer of post-mortem examination.
references: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG). Management of stillbirth. Obstetric Care Consensus No. 10. Washington (DC): ACOG; 2020. Available from: https://www.acog.org/-/media/project/acog/acogorg/clinical/files/obstetric-care-consensus/articles/2020/03/management-of-stillbirth.pdf

Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RANZCOG). Perinatal mortality. Melbourne: RANZCOG; 2021. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/Clinical-Practice-Guideline-for-Perinatal-Mortality.pdf
